coredata.dc:title: In-situ embedding of carbon dots in perovskite GdAlO3:Eu3+/Li+ composite: Boosting phosphor stability, optical thermometry, flexible displays and data protection applications

coredata.dc:description: Phosphors that are highly fluorescent have shown considerable promise in applications like flexible displays and enhancing data security. Still, the task of boosting their fluorescence attributes, especially in the realm of phosphors, remains an ongoing challenge. Precisely, the luminescent properties of GdAlO3:Eu3+ (GAO:Eu3+) phosphors co-doped with lanthanide (Ln) and alkali ions (M+) are successfully synthesized using a solution combustion method. The integration of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) onto the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor surface notably enhanced their luminescent characteristics. Incorporating CDs into GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ resulted in a decrease in both positive and negative defects within the phosphor, consequently enhancing its crystalline structure. Particularly noteworthy is the substantial spectral alignment observed between the emission of CDs and the absorption of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+. When excited by 395 nm UV light, the GAO phosphors displayed the distinctive emission associated with the doping ions. The emission intensity of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+, and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite rises by 18 and 66.23 folds, respectively, in comparison to the corresponding samples lacking the inclusion of CDs. The enhanced photoluminescence (PL) intensity observed can be attributed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. In comparison to its emission intensity at room temperature, the CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite retains 95.6% of its intensity, while GAO:7Eu3+ retains 88.5% and GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ retains 91.2% at 400 K, showcasing remarkable thermal stability. The finely optimized CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ combination displays an impressive color purity (CP) of 98.15 %. Furthermore, this NCs demonstrates significant potential for optical thermometry across a broad temperature spectrum ranging from 300 to 480 K. The brush mode is utilized to create anti-counterfeiting (AC) labels using the optimized composite ink on various surfaces. The resulting AC tags exhibit high resolution and durability. The fabricated flexible films exhibit remarkable flexibility, and foldability significantly broaden the scope of potential applications in the field of AC. These results suggest that CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ have potential for multifaceted applications in flexible luminescent films, AC, and optical thermometry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphors that are highly fluorescent have shown considerable promise in applications like flexible displays and enhancing data security. Still, the task of boosting their fluorescence attributes, especially in the realm of phosphors, remains an ongoing challenge. Precisely, the luminescent properties of GdAlO3:Eu3+ (GAO:Eu3+) phosphors co-doped with lanthanide (Ln) and alkali ions (M+) are successfully synthesized using a solution combustion method. The integration of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) onto the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor surface notably enhanced their luminescent characteristics. Incorporating CDs into GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ resulted in a decrease in both positive and negative defects within the phosphor, consequently enhancing its crystalline structure. Particularly noteworthy is the substantial spectral alignment observed between the emission of CDs and the absorption of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+. When excited by 395 nm UV light, the GAO phosphors displayed the distinctive emission associated with the doping ions. The emission intensity of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+, and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite rises by 18 and 66.23 folds, respectively, in comparison to the corresponding samples lacking the inclusion of CDs. The enhanced photoluminescence (PL) intensity observed can be attributed to the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. In comparison to its emission intensity at room temperature, the CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite retains 95.6% of its intensity, while GAO:7Eu3+ retains 88.5% and GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ retains 91.2% at 400 K, showcasing remarkable thermal stability. The finely optimized CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ combination displays an impressive color purity (CP) of 98.15 %. Furthermore, this NCs demonstrates significant potential for optical thermometry across a broad temperature spectrum ranging from 300 to 480 K. The brush mode is utilized to create anti-counterfeiting (AC) labels using the optimized composite ink on various surfaces. The resulting AC tags exhibit high resolution and durability. The fabricated flexible films exhibit remarkable flexibility, and foldability significantly broaden the scope of potential applications in the field of AC. These results suggest that CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ have potential for multifaceted applications in flexible luminescent films, AC, and optical thermometry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent materials doped with lanthanide ions are garnering significant interest for their potential in lighting, displays, probes, solar cells, and biomedical imaging. Despite the anticipation for enhanced luminescent properties, their luminescence, primarily from electronic transitions within the 4f orbital, is often restricted by parity selection rules. This leads to low molar absorption coefficients and narrow absorption bands, causing inefficient optical excitation and reduced luminescence intensity. To address this, considerable effort is directed towards enhancing their luminescence performance, often through surface ligand modification. The strong absorbance by ligand and effective energy transfer to lanthanide ions offset the low molar absorptivity, boosting the efficiency of luminescent materials doped with Ln3+ ions. This compensatory mechanism enhances the luminescent performance of materials despite their low absorption coefficients [1–4].

Encasing core nanocrystals with an inorganic shell reduces surface-related energy loss, enhancing energy transfer and luminescence. Plasmon resonance can also boost luminescence, but its effectiveness depends on factors like metal NP shape, size, composition, and spatial distance from the luminescent material. Research has focused on enhancing Ln3+ ion luminescence, but there's limited exploration into using fluorescent CDs to improve luminescent properties in Ln3+ ion-doped nano/micro-materials [5–7].

Quasi-zero-dimensional carbon materials like CDs and their composites are under scrutiny for energy storage and photocatalysis. CDs offer significant advantages: large surface area, customizable heteroatom doping, biocompatibility, strong fluorescence, light stability, low toxicity, and functional groups like –OH and –COOH. These properties enhance surface functionalities and stabilities of other nanomaterials, improving their physical and chemical properties. Recent advances have introduced new methods to enhance phosphor luminescence. One approach doubled the phosphorescence efficiency of SrAl2O4:Eu, Dy phosphors by growing a textured layer of crystalline anisotropic material on sapphire [8]. Additionally, integrating CDs into phosphors significantly improved their luminous efficiency, such as applying a blue CD coating to enhance the colorimetric properties of silicate and tellurite glasses doped with Dy3+ ions [9]. CDs@ZnGa2O4 composites showed significantly enhanced PL intensity due to increased disorder and higher vacancy concentration [10]. The study investigated how incorporating carbon dots (CDs) affects phosphor luminescence, focusing on the energy transfer mechanism between CDs and the phosphor. This research aimed to comprehensively understand how CDs enhance phosphor luminescence through detailed exploration of energy transfer processes. Specifically, the luminescence of YF3:Eu3+/CDs NCs was greatly enhanced by energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ions. Similarly, yNCDs composites exhibited increased luminescence due to energy transfer between NCDs and Tm3+ ions [11]. This finding indicates that NCDs significantly enhance luminescence in the composite material through effective energy transfer with Tm3+ ions. Additionally, CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ showed higher PL intensity compared to YBO3:Eu3+, attributed to the FRET mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ ions [12]. In this study, CDs are incorporated into GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ to boost phosphor PL intensity. The investigation analyzed crystallinity, surface morphology, PL characteristics, and thermal stability of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphors with and without CDs. Additionally, the study explored the optical thermometry capabilities of the CDs(7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor.

Rare-earth-activated phosphors are now widely used in information security measures. Compared to existing methods like RFID tags, AC paper, and optical labels with organic dyes, phosphor-based films offer unique advantages: affordability, simple design, non-toxicity, transparency, easy inspection, strong protection, and high efficiency. These attributes make them compelling alternatives for authentication and security purposes. The integration of these phosphors with soluble PDMS has led to the creation of security ink and transparent AC films. PDMS is notable for its sustainability due to its environmentally friendly nature, biodegradability, and superior light transmittance. The resulting composite film exhibits outstanding transparency, flexibility, foldability, scalability, and efficiency, making it a promising alternative to petroleum-based products in the authentication and security domain [13–15].

Phosphor-converted white LEDs (pc-WLEDs) are widely used for indoor and outdoor lighting and as LCD backlights. Commercial pc-WLEDs typically feature a blue LED chip coated with garnet yellow phosphor activated with Ce3+, blending blue and yellow light to produce white light [16]. Indeed, the absence of a red component in blue LED-excited w-LEDs might lead to lower colour rendering index (CRI) and higher correlated colour temperature (CCT), which may not be suitable for certain applications. Introducing a red phosphor can address these issues by adding the red component to the emitted light, thereby improving overall CP and rendering. Eu3+-activated nitride red phosphors are well-regarded luminescent materials, reaching commercialization, but their luminescence extending into the near-infrared can lead to reabsorption issues in the visible range. Addressing this challenge is crucial for improving their performance in various applications. Mn4+activated fluoride red phosphors are highly esteemed for their optimal luminescent range of 600–650 nm, making them top-tier red phosphors. This spectrum enhances light efficiency in warm w-LEDs. However, overcoming challenges like pollution during preparation and susceptibility to moisture requires comprehensive solutions. Eu3+-activated oxide red phosphors can emit red light and efficiently convert wavelengths from near-ultraviolet (nUV) to red, making them a promising alternative for red-emitting phosphors. Moreover, Eu3+-activated oxide red phosphors provide stable emission color, excellent corrosion resistance, eco-friendliness, and easy preparation, driving ongoing interest and research among scientists exploring Eu3+-activated luminescent materials [17,18].

Choosing appropriate host materials is vital as they significantly affect the emission of doped RE ions. Commonly used host materials for RE-doped red-emitting phosphors include molybdates, silicates, tungstates, fluorides, and germinates. However, many of these chemicals require a reduced environment for synthesis, restricting their suitability for large-scale commercial manufacturing. RE-doped aluminate hosts are extensively studied for their exceptional properties and stability. Compounds like GAO and similar perovskite structures derived from rare-earth aluminate are crucial in applications like lasers and magneto-optics [19]. They emit light when interacting with ionizing particles, making them scintillators, with GAO falling under the category of luminescent materials.

Red-emitting (Eu3+) ions have garnered significant interest among all RE ions due to their potential for use in doped phosphors in solid-state lighting. Table S1compares GAO with various dopants [20–37]. Eu3+ ions exhibit two distinct peaks in electronic transitions at 592 nm (5D0 → 7F1) and 615 nm (5D0 → 7F2). Eu3+ emits red light within the human eyes detectable spectrum, making it preferable to Eu3 + and Gd3+, whose emitted red light may not always fall within the visible range. Additionally, the nUV absorption peak of Eu3+ ions at around 395 nm aligns well with the emission wavelength of commonly used blue-emitting GaN chips. This alignment promotes efficient energy transfer between Eu3+ ions and GaN chips, enhancing system performance. These characteristics collectively make Eu3+ ions an ideal choice for developing phosphors that emit red light. So far, numerous studies have been conducted on phosphors doped with Eu3+, including Na5YSi4O12:Eu3+ [38], SrGd2O4:Eu3+ [39], Ca3La7(SiO4)5(PO4)O2:Eu3+ [40], La2Zr2O7:Eu3+ [41], and BaTiO3:Eu3+ [42]. However, the red emission intensity in these host materials is often limited, hindering their widespread use in w-LED devices. Researchers have investigated methods to boost Eu3+ red emission, such as annealing the phosphor at high temperatures and introducing suitable sensitizer ions through co-doping.

In this study, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphors were efficiently synthesized using a simple combustion method. The researchers examined the phase structure, morphology, and luminescent properties of the phosphors. Importantly, the integration of CDs notably enhanced the PL characteristics of the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor. The PL emission intensities of CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li + are boosted by 66.23 times compared to phosphor without impregnated CDs. The study investigates the luminescence enhancement mechanism by analyzing the energy levels of both Eu3+ ions and CDs. This research introduces a synthesis method for innovative composites and offers a promising approach to enhancing the luminescent efficiency of RE-doped phosphors.

Gd(NO3)3 (99.9% purity), Aluminium nitrate Al(NO3)3 (98%), Europium (III) nitrite Eu(NO3)3. (99.9%), Lithium nitrite Li(NO3)3 (99.99%), Calcium nitrate (CaNO3)2 (99.95%), Bismuth nitrate (BiNO3)3 (99.99%), Citric acid (HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2) 99.0 %, Urea (NH2CONH2) 99.5% and PDMS are procured from Sigma Aldrich, India.

The method outlined elsewhere is utilized to prepare Spirulina leaf extract. Initially, a designated amount of dry Spirulina powder is combined with ethanol in a ratio of 1:40, the solution is agitated at a temperature of 60 °C for a duration of 90 min, after which it is permitted to gradually return to room temperature (RT), reaching ambient conditions. Following this, the mixture undergoes filtration and decolorization utilizing activated carbon. Subsequently, another filtration step is conducted to eliminate the activated carbon, and the resulting filtrate is diluted with deionized (DI) water, typically at a ratio of 1:10. The resulting extract is then preserved at a temperature of −4 °C until it is prepared for subsequent utilization. Fig. 1 a shows the extraction procedure of Spirulina platensis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GAO3:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) and GAO3:Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ phosphor
ce:section.ce:para: A series of GAO:7Eu3+ co-doped with Li+ (monovalent), Ca2+ (divalent), and Bi3+ (trivalent) ions, are synthesized following the procedure of the solution combustion method, depicted in Fig. 1b. Equal quantities of the precursors are dissolved in deionized (DI) water, resulting in the formation of a transparent solution known as the redox mixture. Following this, the transparent redox mixture is transferred to a muffle furnace maintained at a temperature of 400 °C for a duration of 15 min to initiate the combustion process, resulting in the release of combustion gases. Within a timeframe of 5 min, the solution undergoes a transition, yielding a frothy GAO:Eu3+ phosphor. To enhance crystallinity and eliminate carbon residues, the resulting powder is then subjected to rough crushing and heating in air at 900 °C for a duration of 3 h.

CDs are synthesized via the hydrothermal technique. A solution is created in a beaker, containing 2.1 g of citric acid and 0.094 g of urea (0.1–1 wt %) dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. Following agitation of the solution on a magnetic stirrer for 5 min at ambient temperature, it is transferred into a 100 mL stainless steel reactor equipped with a Teflon liner for a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 4 h. Following the cooling process to reach RT, the solution undergoes filtration using a 0.22 μm filter to eliminate larger particles present in the solution. Subsequently, the solution is moved into a 20 mL dialysis kit featuring a 1 kDa molecular weight cut-off and undergoes dialysis in 1.5 L of distilled water, repeating the process eight times within 48 h. Consequently, a pale-yellow solution of CDs is acquired. The synthesis process of CDs by utilizing urea and citric acid is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

The synthesis started by combining 10 mL of distilled water with urea (2 g) and citric acid (1 g), followed by stirring the mixture for 15 min. Afterward, 0.05 gm of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ NPs, which had been dissolved in 3 ml of water beforehand, are introduced into the mixture. The resulting mixture is vigorously agitated for an additional 15 min before being transferred into a 100 mL autoclave to undergo a reaction process for 8 h at a temperature of 200 °C. Following cooling, the resulting product underwent washing with ethanol and subsequent centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 3 cycles. It's notable that the CDs are directly integrated onto the surface of GAO:Eu3+/Li+, removing the necessity for a dialysis step. Afterward, the synthesized sample is dried at 80 °C for 6 h to ready it for additional analysis and applications. To enhance the luminescent properties of GAO:Eu3+ phosphors, fluorescent CDs are integrated into the structure to form a composite of CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+. The synthesis of CDs and the fabrication process of CDs@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ NCs are illustrated schematically in Fig. 3 .

The w-LED incorporates the nUV chip (λ = 395 nm) along with the red phosphor synthesized in this study, alongside commercially available green ((Ba, Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+) and blue (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+) phosphors. The phosphors are combined in the mixture with a weight ratio of 8:1:3, respectively. The electroluminescence (EL) properties of the w-LED are measured using a spectrometer (USB 4000, Ocean Optics, USA).

In order to achieve the target dynamic viscosity, 0.25 g of the CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:Eu3+/Li+ composite is carefully blended into an ethanol-water-glycerol solution at a ratio of 30:7.5. This mixture is then augmented with sodium dodecyl sulfonate (3 mg/L) to adjust the surface tension of the ink, while Polydimethylsiloxane is introduced as a solid binder. For the production of translucent ink, this blend undergoes ultrasonic processing for approximately 45 min. The ink obtained is employed for printing AC patterns on various surfaces through a method similar to traditional screen printing. These patterns are subsequently observed in real-time while exposed to both conventional light and UV 365 nm light irradiation. The detailed synthesis procedure is discussed elsewhere [43].

To fabricate the phosphor-polymer composite film, a total of 0.9 g of Polydimethylsiloxane is added to 50 mL of deionized (DI) water. Subsequently, the mixture is heated on a hot plate at 100 °C while being continuously stirred until the PDMS is completely dissolved. The polymer slurry is mixed with 0.1 g of CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:Eu3+/Li+ phosphor, and the resulting mixture underwent sonication for a duration of 1 h to ensure thorough blending and achieve a uniform consistency. This blend is then poured into a Petri dish and allowed to solidify at room temperature for 1 day. The detailed fabrication process of NC films is discussed elsewhere [44].

The following instruments are utilized for characterizing the prepared samples.
Sl No Characterization technique Name of the instrument and specification Purpose

Fig. 4 a depicts the PXRD patterns of undoped and GAO:Eu3+(1–11 mol %) phosphor calcined at 900 °C for 3 h, respectively. The diffraction patterns are accurately aligned with the pure orthorhombic phase of GAO with a Pbnm (62) space group, as per the standard JCPDS file no. 46–0395 [45]. Fig. 4b The XRD patterns for the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor co-doped with Bi3+, Ca2+, and Li+ ions all exhibit an orthorhombic crystal structure typical of GAO. Importantly, no additional impurity peaks are observed, suggesting that the inclusion of Li+, Ca2+, and Bi3+ ions does not introduce any undesired phases or impurities into the crystal structure. The Rietveld refinement technique is utilized to ascertain the structural parameters for all samples. Intriguingly, there are no notable alterations observed in the structural parameters upon doping with Li+ (monovalent), Ca2+ (divalent), and Bi3+ (trivalent) ions into GAO:7Eu3+. This indicates that the Li+, Ca2+ and Bi3+ co-dopant ions effectively substituted the Gd3+ sites in GAO. The XRD pattern of the CDs displays broad peaks located at 2θ = 19.27° and 40.81°, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes of amorphous carbon, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. Additionally, the distinct diffraction peaks characteristic of both GAO and CDs are concurrently detected in the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor, as depicted in Fig. 4c. The percentage difference (Dr) between the radius of the host cation (Gd3+) and the substituted ion (Eu3+) is calculated using the formula [46]:
(1)
D r
=
[
R h
(
C N
)
−
R d
(

)
]
R h
(
C N
)
× 100
%
Where Rh represents the radius of the Gd3+ ion and Rd represents the radius of the Eu3+ ion and the coordination number is denoted by CN. This percentage difference provides insight into the extent of substitution and its impact on the crystal lattice structure. In the case of a Eu3+doped GAO system, the percentage difference in radius is found to be 9.52 %. This is because smaller Eu3+ ions (with a coordination number of 9 and a radius of 0.95 Å) replaced larger Gd3+ ions (also with a coordination number of 9, but with a radius of 1.05 Å). The generally accepted range for the percentage deviation in ionic radius between host ions and substituted ions is typically considered to be within 30%. In the case of doped Eu3+ ions in the GAO host matrix, where the radius percentage difference is approximately 9.52 %, this result suggests that the Eu3+ ions effectively occupy the Gd3+ positions within the GAO host matrix.

The Rietveld refinement technique, implemented with GSAS software [47], is employed to refine the structural parameters of GAO, GAO:7Eu3+, and GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ samples, utilizing PXRD data (Fig. 4d–f). The analysis revealed that all synthesized samples exhibited crystallization in the orthorhombic structure, characterized by the space group Pbnm (62). The refined parameters are outlined in Table 1 . Subsequently, the crystal structure of both GAO:7Eu3+ and GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ compounds is constructed using the Rietveld-refined structural parameters, utilizing the VESTA program. In Fig. 4g and h, the less symmetrical or distorted orthorhombic crystal structure of GAO is depicted. Within this perovskite structure, 8 oxygen atoms are bonded to Gd3+ ions in a distorted square-antiprismatic environment, whereas Al3+ is octahedrally coordinated to 6 oxygen atoms in a centrosymmetric arrangement, forming an AlO6 octahedron. The atomic parameters of GAO with an orthorhombic unit cell are compiled and presented in Table 1 for reference and analysis.

XPS analysis is employed to determine the chemical composition and charge state of the GAO and CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite. Fig. 5 a illustrates the XPS survey spectra acquired from GAO. In Fig. 5b, the Gd 3d spectrum displays discernible peaks at 1218.1 and 1187.0 eV, which affirm the presence of Gd³⁺ valence state. Fig. 5c presents normalization utilizing the Al 2p binding energy peak positioned at 73.29 eV. In Fig. 5d, the O 1s spectrum demonstrates an oxidation state of −2, reflected in binding energies of 529.5, 531.0, and 532.6 eV. The XPS survey spectra of the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite are depicted in Fig. 5e. Peaks in the survey scan are assigned using the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) XPS database. No impurities are detected in the composite aside from carbon. Experimental peaks corresponding to Gd 3d core electrons are distinctly observed as a pair at 1186.4 and 1218.3 eV [48]. Most of our binding energy (BE) values correspond to Gd and closely align with those found in other Gd3+ compounds. The carbon peak observed may be attributed to surface carbon absorption, which occurs during the exposure of the composite to air, as illustrated in Fig. 5f. The core level peak of the Al 2p signal is observed at 73.0 eV [49], as shown in Fig. 5g. Both the electron core levels of Gd 4d and Al 2p are well resolved. In Fig. 5h, the O 1s peak is deconvoluted into three distinct peaks. Peak 1 is attributed to the presence of the –OH group located on the surface of the material, whereas peak 2 corresponds to oxygen vacancies within the structure. Peak 3 is associated with lattice oxygen atoms present in the crystal lattice. These assignments provide valuable insights into the composition and structural composition of the material [50]. In Fig. 5i, the peaks observed at around 1170.6 and 1134.9 eV correspond to the 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 core levels of Eu3+, respectively. The Eu3+ core level spectra reveal the presence of europium in its +3 oxidation state [51]. Additionally, the peak observed at 56.9 eV can be attributed to Li 1s [52], as depicted in Fig. 5j, confirming the +1 oxidation state of lithium. The deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1s spectrum unveiled two distinct peaks at 284.7 eV and 289.2 eV [53], assigned to the sp3 bulk-bonded carbon (C–C) and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 5k). This examination suggests the existence of –OH and –COOH groups on the surface of the CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite, thereby affirming the effective integration of CDs onto the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite.

FE-SEM technique was used to understand the morphology of GAO:Eu3+ (1–11mol %) phosphors, Bi3+, Ca2+ and Li + co-doped GAO:7Eu3+ composite (Fig. 6 a–j). The FE-SEM images of GAO:Eu3+/Bi3+/Ca2+/Li+ phosphors showed uneven, fused and dumbbell shaped particles (Fig. 6a–j). Fig. 6k displays the FE-SEM image of CDs distinctly exhibit spherical particles with well-defined edges. Fig. 6l shows the FE-SEM image of CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite clearly showed the CDs are embedded on the phosphor (enlarged view of Fig. 6l). From the using particle size distribution histograms, the average particle size was estimated and shown in Fig. 6m-p. As can be seen from the histograms, the estimated particle size is found to be ∼0.28 μm (GAO:7Eu3+) and ∼4.22 nm (CDs) respectively. However, in the case of CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite the average particle size for phosphor and CDs is found to be ∼0.32 μm and ∼5.47 nm respectively.

The microscopic properties of the carbon dots were determined using TEM analysis and are shown in Fig. 7 a. The results indicate that the particles are spherical, homogeneously monodispersed, and with no form of aggregation. The estimated particle size is ∼3.4 nm, and the size distribution histogram (Fig. 7b) presents a narrow size distribution of the CDs, which also attests to the size of the CDs. The HR-TEM image (inset of Fig. 7a) showed distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the (100) of the lattice planes of CDs and implies high crystallinity of the CDs. Fig. 7c shows the TEM image of GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor clearly reveals fused particles with size ∼ 7–10 nm. In addition, the size distribution is used to estimate the average particle size and is found to be 7 nm (Fig. 7d). The TEM image of CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is shown in Fig. 7e and the corresponding average particle size of CDs in composite is found to be 5.5 nm (Fig. 7f). The HR-TEM image of CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is depicted in Fig. 7g and the average inter-planar distance (d) of the composite material is 0.22 nm, which is located in the (100) plane of CDs. The large number of carboxylic acid components that CDs typically have at their surface gives them a high affinity for water. They are ideal for further integration with other polymeric, organic, inorganic, or biologically active substances. From TEM image of CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite, tight adherence of the individual CDs to the surface of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite can be seen, which is clearly explained by the binding between carboxylic acid components and composite, thus distinguishing the boundaries between CDs and the surface of phosphor could be done. Further, the lattice spacing (d) between lattice planes of phosphor, CDs and phosphor are estimated and found to be is ∼0.22, 0.263 and 0.214 nm respectively (Fig. 7h–j). In addition, the lattice spacing (d), calculated using GATAN software [54], revealed values of 0.22, 0.263 and 0.214 nm for (100), (220) and (103) planes, respectively (Fig. 7k-m). Significantly, the inter-planar d-spacing derived from HR-TEM images exactly matches the GATAN software results.

Fig. 8 a illustrates the excitation spectrum of the GAO:Eu3+ phosphor, which is recorded at an emission wavelength of 611 nm. This spectrum showcases a broad band within the 200–340 nm range, as well as a series of sharp bands spanning from 350 to 500 nm. The broad band with a peak at 277 nm is attributed to the charge transfer from O2− ions to Eu3+ ions. The sharp peaks observed at 317 nm (7F0→5H6), 359 nm (7F0→5D4), 382 nm (7F0→5G2), 392 nm (7F0→5L6), 404 nm (7F0→5D3), and 462 nm (7F0→5D2) correspond to the 4f-4f transitions of Eu3+ ions. The excitation spectra reveal stronger intensities for the peaks centered at 277 nm and 392 nm, indicating that GAO:Eu3+ phosphors can be effectively excited by nUV radiation. Among these transitions, the most prominent peak at 392 nm corresponds to the 7F0→5L6 transition, making it an optimal excitation wavelength that aligns well with UV chip characteristics [55]. Consequently, the synthesized GAO:7Eu3+ phosphors hold significant promise for UV-based w-LED applications. Fig. 8b depicts the PL spectra of GAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %) phosphors monitored at 395 nm excitation. Several distinct peaks are noted within the 500–750 nm range, stemming from 5D0- 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively) electronic transitions of Eu3+ ions. Specifically, the transition at 592 nm (5D0→7F1) represents a magnetic-dipole (MD) transition that remains unaffected by the host environment. The transitions at 615 nm (5D0→7F2) and 698 nm (5D0→7F4) are attributed to induced electric-dipole (ED) transitions. Additionally, the transition at 653 nm (5D0→7F3) constitutes a forbidden transition, not complying with the selection rules of either ED or MD transitions, thus its intensity can be disregarded [56].

The transition from 5D0 to 7F2 displayed significant sensitivity to the local coordination environment of Eu3+ ions, primarily attributed to the ED intensity considerations. In contrast, the strength of the crystal field surrounding the Eu3+ ions minimally impact the magnetic-dipole (MD) force associated with the accepted 5D0→7F1 transition. The emission peaks exhibited splitting, which can be attributed to factors such as crystal field symmetry, composition, and coordination environment. This phenomenon, known as the Stark effect, arises from the division of excited energy levels of rare-earth ions into various sublevels by the crystal field during the 5D0→7F2 transition. This division enhances the probability of transitions occurring near the anticipated wavelength, resulting in extra peaks in the spectrum [57]. Fig. 8c demonstrates the integrated intensity plotted against the Eu3+ doping concentration. The luminescence intensity of GAO:Eu3+ phosphors increases gradually as the concentration of Eu3+ doping rises, reaching a peak at 7 mol%. However, beyond this concentration, the emission intensity shows a slight decrease instead of further increase. This occurrence is attributed to concentration quenching. For a deeper exploration of the interaction mechanism between Eu3+ ions at elevated doping levels, the critical distance (Rc) is calculated using the formula outlined in equation (2):
(2)
R c
∼ 2
(
3 V
4
χ c
π Z
)
1 3
In this expression, V denotes the volume of the cell, Z represents the count of substitutable cations within the unit cell, and χc signifies the optimal concentration of Eu3+ doping. Specifically, for the GAO:7Eu3+ system, the parameters are V = 117.37 Å³, χc = 7 mol %, and N = 2. The critical value Rc = 20.23 Å delineates a crucial boundary, clearly distinguishing exchange interaction from multipole-multipole interaction. When Rc exceeds 5, the crystal interaction is classified as multipole-multipole interaction; otherwise, it is deemed exchange interaction. The Rc value of the critical distance is calculated to be approximately 20.23 Å using the equation provided. Hence, the concentration quenching phenomenon observed in GAO:7Eu3+ is attributed to multipole-multipole interaction. Moreover, to thoroughly explore the relationship between luminescence intensity and Eu3+ concentration, Dexter introduced the multipole interaction mechanism as illustrated in Equation (3):
(3)
I x
= k
[
1 + β
( x )
Q / 3
]
− 1

Under similar excitation conditions, the constants K and β remain consistent for the GAO matrix. The variable I denotes the luminescence intensity emitted by GAO:7Eu3+ upon excitation, where x represents the concentration of doped Eu3+ ions. This relationship allows for the characterization of luminescence behavior as a function of dopant concentration within the GAO matrix. The values of Q are associated with four distinct types of interactions (Q = 3, 6, 8, 10), each signifying a different category of multipolar interaction: nearest-neighbor ions (Q = 3), dipole-dipole (Q = 6), dipole-quadrupole (Q = 8), and quadrupole-quadrupole interaction (Q = 10). Fig. 8d illustrates a plot of lg(I/x) versus lgx for excitation at 395 nm. The slope (-Q/3) closely aligns with −2.32, indicating an approximate Q value of 8.69, which is in close proximity to 8. Hence, the concentration-quenching mechanism in GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor is attributed to dipole-quadrupole (q-q) interaction [58].

Enhancing the PL performance involves integrating co-dopant ions (sensitizer ions) into the Gd3+ sites within the host lattice of the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor through a charge compensation technique. The impact of co-dopant ions, possessing Li+, Ca2+, and Bi3+ cation charges, on the emission intensity of PL is examined. The PL spectra of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ are recorded under an excitation wavelength of 395 nm (Fig. 8e). The PL emission intensity, associated with the 5D0→7F2 transition, is examined as a function of co-dopant ions in the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphors, excited at λexci = 395 nm. Comparing to the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor, the PL spectra of co-doped phosphors are augmented by 18, 2.6, and 1.8 folds, respectively (Fig. 8e). It is observed that the intensity of red emission rises with a reduction in ionic radii, while it is independent of the decrease in cationic charge (Fig. 8f). In this situation, the variation in ionic radii results in different sub-lattice structures surrounding the luminescent center, thereby impacting the spin-orbit coupling and crystal field of the Eu3+ ions. As a result, this influences the overall coordination environment surrounding the Eu3+ ions. Additionally, the Eu–O bond distance fluctuates depending on the ionic radius of the co-dopant ions, leading to changes in peak intensity.

Fig. 9 a presents the PL spectra of the prepared CDs. The PLE spectrum of the CDs exhibits a wide band with a peak at 330 nm, while the emission spectrum reveals a broad band with a peak at 408 nm. These characteristics are indicative of the typical luminescent properties of CDs, characterized by broad excitation and emission spectra mainly originating from the surface states of the CDs. Upon examining the synthesis process illustrated in Fig. S5, it is evident that citric acid and urea underwent ionization in the solution initially, resulting in the formation of polymer-like CDs through a dehydration-condensation reaction. Following this, the polymer-like CDs underwent carbonization, leading to the formation of the CDs. Consequently, the resulting CDs showcase a significant presence of functional groups (such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2, etc.) on their surfaces, as confirmed by the FTIR spectrum (Figs. S3a–d), which will be discussed further in subsequent sections. These functional groups have the potential to aid in the integration of CDs with the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor. Throughout the synthesis process, the suspension of GAO:7Eu3+ precursors is combined with a solution of CDs. The functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 located on the surface of the CDs may interact with the –OH groups present on the surface of the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor. After hydrothermal treatment, the CDs are incorporated onto the surface of the GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor, leading to the creation of the CDs@GAO:7Eu3+ composite. Notably, the photoluminescence intensity of the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+ composite is considerably higher than that of GAO:7Eu3+/Li+. The phenomenon can be elucidated by the occurrence of FRET between the CDs and Eu3+ ions. Theoretical considerations suggest three fundamental prerequisites for FRET [59]: First, the acceptor's absorbance spectrum must overlap with the luminescence emission spectrum of the donor. Additionally, the energy transfer takes place within the spectral cross-section region illustrated in Fig. 9b. Typically, the orientations of the transition dipoles of both the donor and acceptor need to be parallel. Through the dipole-dipole interaction between the transition dipole moments of the acceptor and donor, energy transfer via FRET can occur. When absorbing light, the donor CDs are raised to an excited state, leading to two potential outcomes: either non-radiative energy transfer to the acceptor (Eu3+), or radiative relaxation through fluorescence. The mechanism driving the enhancement of luminescence can be understood through the phenomenon where, upon excitation at a wavelength of 395 nm, CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li + experiences the transition of electrons in Eu3+ from the 7F2 state to various components of the excited 5d energy level. Concurrently, electrons in CDs transition from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) with the absorbed photon energy, as depicted in Fig. 9c. Upon excitation, the excited electrons relax to both core and surface state energies, subsequently transitioning to the 7F0-4 energy levels of Eu3+ through the FRET mechanism. The electrons transfer to 7F0 via non-radiative relaxation, resulting in the emission of red light from the ground states of Eu3+. Table S3offers a comparative summary of the improved PL observed in different CDs composite [60–74]. The emission intensity of the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite exhibited a remarkable increase of 66.23 times compared to that of the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor (Fig. 9d). The change in the FWHM value indicates an improvement in the crystallinity of CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite. This change is likely due to a decrease in positive and negative defects within the material resulting from the incorporation of CDs, which triggered an oxidation/reduction effect.

Color coordinates serve as crucial indices for assessing the performance of phosphors and NCs. Fig. 9e shows the CIE chromaticity coordinates of GAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %), GAO:7Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ and (1–7 wt %) CDs@GAO:Eu3+/Li+ composite. As can be seen from the figure, the CIE coordinates of GAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %), GAO:7Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ are located in orange-red region. Whereas, addition of CDs into the phosphor, the CIE coordinates are slight shifted from orange-red to bright red. The obtained CIE coordinate values are listed in Table 2 . In addition, the CP of the phosphor and composite can be obtained from Equation (4) [75]:

where (xs, ys) are the chromaticity coordinates of the prepared phosphor with different concentrations, (xi, yi) is the CIE coordinate of the white reference light equal to (0.333, 0.333), and (xd, yd) is the coordinate of the intersection of the line formed by the above two points and the boundary. For the optimized composite, the corresponding (xi, yi), (xs, ys), and (xd, yd) are (0.333, 0.333), (0.610, 0.3452), and (0.6101 0.3452), respectively. The CP of the GAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %), GAO:7Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ is found to be in the range 85.50–89.50 %, whereas, optimized (7 wt %)CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite reached to 98.00 %, suggests the potential utility of the CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li + phosphor for encapsulating w-LED. The estimated CP values for both the phosphor and composite are presented in Table 2. The findings suggest that the composite exhibits favorable color stability. The CCT serves as a widely adopted metric for characterizing the nature of luminescent color. The CCT values for both the phosphor and composite can be determined using Equation (5):
(5) CCT = − 449n3 + 3625n2 − 6823.3n + 5520.33
where n = (x-xe)/(y-ye) and (xe, ye) = (0.332, 0.186). Fig. 9f shows the CCT plots of GAO:Eu3+ (1–11 mol %), GAO:7Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ and (1–7 wt%) CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite and calculated CCTs values of phosphor and the composite are provided in Table 2. The obtained CCT values are in the range 1091–1165 K which are <3000 K, indicating that the composite is quite useful in warm white light applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of radiative intensity parameters (Ω2, Ω4)
ce:section.ce:para:
The Judd-Ofelt theory explores the structural arrangement of the immediate surroundings of Eu3+ ions within the GAO lattice, analyzing radiative intensity parameters (Ω2, Ω4) extracted from emission spectra to understand the influence of the local environment on the luminescent behavior. Furthermore, the Judd-Ofelt parameter facilitates the calculation of radiative transition probability (A), branching ratio (β), and relative decay time. However, in this instance, Ω6 and A0−6 cannot be computed because the 5D0→7F6 transition is absent in the emission spectra. The JO parameter corresponding to the 5D0→7F2 transition, known as the covalency parameter (Ω2), is a hypersensitive transition with an asymmetry ratio greater than 1 [76]. Ω4 is related to the electron density around the ligands. The value of the ratio Ω2/Ω4 of the GAO:7Eu3+/Li+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+/Ca2+/Bi3+ and CDs(7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+, a value greater than one suggests the occupation of dopant ions in an asymmetric environment within the host matrix. This elevated radiative rate results in enhanced luminescent properties, thereby rendering the compound more efficient for LEDs [77]. The radiative emission rate (A0−J) is determined by the combined emission intensities (I0−J and I0−1) originating from various 5D0 and 7FJ (J = 1, 2, and 4) transitions, along with their respective energies (hυ0−J), following the subsequent correlation [78]:

The value of Ω2 is assumed to be contingent upon the covalent properties of the Eu3+ ion's site, primarily due to the hypersensitive 5D0 → 7F2 transition, rendering it structure-sensitive. These Ω2 and Ω4 parameters are indicative of the stiffness and viscosity characteristics of the host matrix. Based on the data presented in Table 3 , it is clear that for all doping and co-doping concentrations, Ω2 surpasses Ω4. This finding implies a covalent interaction between the Eu3+ ions and O2−ligands, signaling an asymmetry surrounding the Eu3+ ions within the GAO system. The elevated asymmetric ratio affirms the hypersensitivity of CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite towards the electric dipole transition (5D0→7F2), emphasizing the dominance of electric dipole transition over magnetic dipole transition (5D0→7F1). The distinct intensity of the 5D0 → 7F2 transition (615 nm) in the PL spectra stems from the elevated Ω2 parameter, suggesting the covalent bond between Eu−O and the substantial asymmetry surrounding Eu3+ ions within the host. This asymmetry ratio serves to reaffirm the notable asymmetry present around the Eu3+ ions. Asymmetry ratios exceeding 1 suggest that Eu3+ occupies highly asymmetric positions and establishes covalent bonds with its surroundings within the solid. Moreover, the branching ratio for the 5D0→7F2 transition surpasses that of other transitions, underscoring its primary role in the emission of Eu3+. The estimated J−O parameters are summarized in Table 3. The comparison of J-O parameters, asymmetric ratio, lifetime values of different phosphors Table 4 [82–87].

Evaluating thermal stability is crucial when considering the potential application of a material in solid-state lighting. The thermal stability of the GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor, and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is investigated using TDPL spectra analysis across a temperature span of 300–480 K under 395 nm excitation wavelength, as depicted in Fig. 10 a–c. As temperature rises, it becomes apparent that both the shape and position of the peak remain constant. Upon reaching 420 K, the residual luminescence intensity of the CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite remains at 95.6% of the luminescence intensity observed at 420 K, which is significantly higher than that of GAO:7Eu3+ (88.5%), GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ (91.2%) phosphor (Fig. 10d), and commercial phosphors Y2O3:Eu3+ (81.98%) and Sr2Si5N8:Eu2+ (81.98%) [88,89]. Table 5 compares the residual temperatures of the present composite with those of commercial phosphors [90–93]. The findings demonstrate the exceptional thermal stability of the CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite. The activation energy (Ea) stands as a pivotal parameter in investigating the thermal quenching phenomenon, computed using the Arrhenius equation as follows [94]:

)
In this expression, K represents the Boltzmann constant (8.629 × 10−5 eV/K), A stands for the constant frequency factor, and the emission intensity at 300 K and temperature T is indicated by I0 and IT, respectively. The linearly fitted ln((I0/It)-1) vs 1/KBT plots of GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor and CDs(7 wt%)@ GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite are presented in Fig. 10e-g. The slope of the each fitted line represents the Ea. The estimated Ea values for phosphor and composite is found to be 0.25, 0.27 and 0.30 eV respectively. Table 6 shows the comparison of Ea values with reported literature [95–100]. As can be seen from the table, the obtained Ea of composite is fairly high (∼0.30 eV), signifying the good thermal stability of the composite.

The phenomenon of thermal quenching in emission can be explained using a configurational coordinate diagram (CCD) shown in Fig. 10h. This diagram presents the Born−Oppenheimer potential energy of the Eu3+ system, with the Ue parabola representing the crystal field transition barrier (CTB) formed by the overlap of the Gd−O and Eu−O. The 5Dj and 7Fj states represent the excited and ground states of the Eu3+ ions, respectively, with each state dependent on a single generalized configuration coordinate R. The horizontal lines marked within the two parabolas represent the energy levels of the vibrational states. Point A, where the 5Dj and Ue parabolas intersect, indicates the crossover point at which the energy of the vibrational states of the 5Dj and Ue electronic states are equal. The thermal activation energy, denoted by ΔE, depicts the energy difference between point A and the lowest point of the 5Dj electronic state. At first, electrons in the 7Fj state absorb the energy from incoming photons and transition to the excited state (5Dj) via the pathway (1) of photoexcitation, thereby perturbing the equilibrium of the system. Subsequently, via a nonradiative pathway, electrons relinquish some of their energy to the lattice, ultimately reaching the lowest point of the 5Dj state. At ambient temperature, electrons revert to the 7Fj state through radiative relaxation, as delineated by path (3). The emitted photon energy tends to be lower than the excitation energy, as some energy is dissipated during lattice relaxation to attain the excited steady state. As temperature rises, lattice vibrations intensify, leading to an increased presence of thermally active phonons. These phonons interact with the excited electrons at the bottom of the 5Dj state, adjusting their energy level to coincide with the crossover point A. Upon reaching point A (via path 2), electrons undergo nonradiative energy dissipation, advancing along path (4) through phonon relaxation. As temperature increases, the interaction between electrons and phonons intensifies, leading to a greater number of electrons relaxing non-radiatively to the ground state [101].

The temperature-dependent behavior of the emission peaks from Eu3+ ions offers potential applications in luminescence thermometry. In this study, we focus on the emission peaks at 592 nm (corresponding to the 5D0→7F1 transition) and 615 nm (representing the 5D0→7F2 transition). Fig. 10a–c displays the TDPL spectra and the normalized intensity variation of the three peaks, respectively. Notably, with increasing temperature, there is a reduction in the intensity of each peak. Nevertheless, the rate of decline varies among the three peaks. This discrepancy in the rate of decrease is harnessed for FIR-based luminescence thermometry. FIR values are calculated for the intensities of the 592 nm and 615 nm peaks using the following equation [102]:
(14)
FIR =
I 1
I 2
=
B exp
( ‐
△ E
K B
T
)
In the equation, I1 denotes the integrated peak intensity at 592 nm, I2 represents the integrated peak intensity at 615 nm, A stands for the proportional parameter, K represents the Boltzmann constant, and B denotes the offset parameter. Fig. 10i–k illustrates the relationship between ln(FIR) and the reciprocal of temperature (1/T) for the FIR values. The change in intensity ratios I590/I615 with temperature is illustrated in the inset of Fig. 10i–k. The slope of the ln(FIR) versus 1/KBT graph provides the ΔE for I590/I615, yielding 0.20 eV (1644 cm−1), 0.21 eV (1720 cm−1) and 0.23 (1855 cm−1) for GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+, and CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite respectively. The thermal sensitivity can be further assessed by determining the absolute and relative sensitivity values through the following pair of equations [103],
(15)
S a

where Sa & Sr indicates the absolute and relative sensitivities, respectively. The Sr and Sa (max) values at 300 K for GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite are found to be (2.62 % K−1 and 4.18 × 10−4 K−1), (2.74 % K−1, 6.10 × 10−4 K−1) and (3.26 % K−1 and 3.42 × 10−4 K−1) respectively (Fig. 10l-n). Table 7 shows the shows the comparison of Sr values with reported literature [104–109].

Fig. 10o shows the CIE chromaticity coordinates of CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:Eu3+/Li+ composite at various temperatures (300–480 K). The CIE chromaticity coordinates exhibit minimal deviation, indicating high color purity. The calculated results of the CIE chromaticity coordinates and color purity are presented in Table S4, with all color purity values exceeding 98 %. The chromaticity shift ΔE of the emission color can be calculated using the following formula:
(17)
△ E =
(
u t ′
−
u 0 ′
)
2

(
v t ′
−
v 0 ′
)

)
2
In this equation, x and y represent the CIE chromaticity coordinates. Additionally, u1 = 4x/(3 + 12y-2x), v1 = 9y/(3 + 12y-2x), and t1 = 1-u1-v1. The chromaticity coordinates at temperature t and RT are denoted as u′0 and u′t, respectively. The computed ΔE for CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite at 420 K is 0.012, which is less than that of commercial YAG:Ce3+ (0.420) [110] and CaAlSiN3: Eu2+ (0.044) phosphors [111]. This result implies that the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite exhibits commendable resistance to color deviation. Meanwhile, Fig. 10p illustrates the variation of the x and y coordinates of CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:Eu3+/Li+ composite from 300 K to 480 K. Upon calculation, the variances Sx 2 and Sy 2 of the CIE coordinates are found to be 0.00131 and 0.00241, respectively. This provides further evidence of the exceptional thermal stability of the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite, making it an excellent material for application in w-LEDs.

Fig. 11 a–d presents the luminescent decay curves illustrating the transfer of energy from CDs to GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ in CDs(7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite. These decay curves can be effectively fitted using single or double exponential functions [112]:
(18)
I =
I 0

(
‐ t / τ
)

A 1

( ‐ t /

)

A 2
exp
( ‐ t /
τ 2
)

Here, I denote the emission intensity at time t, with A1 and A2 representing constants. τ1 and τ2 denote the double exponential components of the decay time. The lifetime of CDs is approximately 5.77 ms (Fig. 11a). Luminescent materials often exhibit double exponential decay behavior due to energy transfer mechanisms. The average lifetime (τ) can be determined using the following equation [113]:
(20)
<
τ
a v g
> =

The decay profile for the samples of CDs, GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is as shown in Fig. 11a–d. The experimental data are fitted with single and bi-exponential curves. The decay time for the CDs, GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is found to be 5.77 ms (τ1), 2.33 ms (τ2) 2.48 (τ3) and 2.74 ms (τ4) respectively. It is observed that the τ values in composite is relatively increased as compared to plain phosphor. This is mainly due to the effect of donor (CDs) –acceptor (Eu3+) pair recombination which results in luminescence. The luminescence process is attributed to the fact that, the wave function of an electron which is trapped at a donor when overlaps to some extent with the wave function of a hole located at an acceptor, both particles can recombine radiatively. The incorporation of CDs in GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ phosphor sample increases the surface area of the composite and when the surface area increases with decrease in particle size, there are more and more defects on the surface, which may act as luminescent centers. Raikwar [114] was studied life time values of CQDs and CQDs@LaPO4:Eu3+ nanocomposite. The decay time for the CQD sample is found to be 2.18 ms. However, for LaPO4:Eu3+ phosphor and CQD@LaPO4:Eu3+ composite is observed to be 1.80 and 2.01 ms respectively. The increased decay time in CQD@LaPO4:Eu3+ composite as compared to LaPO4:Eu3+ phosphor may have the effect of donor–acceptor pair recombination which results in luminescence. Jingbin Huang et al. [115], studied the luminescence enhancement Lanthanide Ions Doped MgWO4 Nanosheets through incorporation of carbon dots. The lifetime of CDs is about 7.73 ns. As for CDs@MgWO4:12%Eu3+ and CDs@ MgWO4:8%Tb3+ nanosheets, the decay curves of CDs luminescence are well-fitted into a double exponential function. The double exponential decay behaviour is usually observed in the luminescent materials occurred energy transfer phenomenon. The average lifetimes of CDs luminescence in CDs@MgWO4:12%Eu3+ and CDs@MgWO4:8%Tb3+ nanosheets are about 2.31 and 2.25 ns respectively, which is lower than that of pure CDs, indicating the existence of energy transfer between CDs and MgWO4:Ln3+ nanosheets. Li Chunyang et al. [116], studied the encapsulation of carbon nanodots in micro-spherical YBO3:Eu3+ phosphors. The luminescent decay curve of YBO3:Eu3+ and CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite can be well fitted to double exponential and the obtained values are 0.27 ms for YBO3:Eu3+ and 1.63 ms for CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ composite, respectively. The lifetime of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ greatly increases as compared with that of YBO3:Eu3+, demonstrating that there exists FRET between CDs and Eu3+ ions.

The IQE of the phosphor plays a crucial role in assessing its suitability for application in white LEDs (w-LEDs). Fig. 11e–g illustrates a comparison between the excitation line of the BaSO4 reference and the emission spectra of GAO:7Eu3+ phosphors, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ and CDs (7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li + composite. The IQE value can be calculated using Equation (21) [117]:
(21)
I
Q E
=
∫
L S
∫
E R
− ∫
E S
Where LS represents the emission of GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ and CDs(7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite, ER and ES denote the excitation spectra of the BaSO4 reference, phosphor and composite respectively. When excited at a wavelength of 395 nm, the internal quantum efficiency (IQE) values of the phosphor and composite reach 68.31 %, 74.32 %, and 81.62 %, respectively. Given that Quantum Efficiency (QE) serves as a pivotal metric for assessing the application efficacy of phosphors, we conduct a comparative analysis of the synthesized phosphor against several previously documented Eu3+ doped phosphors. Illustrated in Fig. 11h, the CD (7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite developed in this study exhibits a superior QE, outperforming those documented in the literature as enumerated in Table 8 [118–121]. These findings demonstrate the suitability of the composite as a material for white LEDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs@GAO:Eu3+/Li+ red phosphor in w-LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the potential application of the synthesized red phosphor in phosphor-converted LEDs (pc-LEDs), a warm w-LED device is assembled. This device employed a 395 nm near-UV LED chip along with commercially available blue phosphor (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+), green phosphor ((Sr, Ba)2SiO4:Eu2+), and the prepared optimized composite (CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+). The blue, green, and red phosphors are mixed in a weight ratio of approximately 2:1:5. Fig. 12 a illustrates the CIE chromaticity coordinates of both the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ red phosphor and the assembled warm white LED device under a 20 mA current excitation. The warm white LED device exhibits a CIE chromaticity coordinate of (0.3218, 0.3233). Fig. 12b displays the EL spectra of the assembled warm white LED, accompanied by a digital photograph inset showcasing the warm white LED [122,123]. Taken together, these results imply that CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ holds promise as a compelling choice for red phosphors in high-power warm white LEDs.

The significance of information storage and security remains constant in society. However, progressing chemical AC systems poses a substantial challenge. A range of fluorescent materials have been utilized in endeavors to combat counterfeiting. However, despite these efforts, challenges such as toxicity, spectral overlap, low quantum efficiency, and luminescence quenching continue to present obstacles [124,125]. Developing AC ink to safeguard security papers from forgery is a challenging task. Nevertheless, integrating CDs into phosphor samples, like the CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite, can produce highly effective AC ink. Fig. 13 a shows the AC labels developed on paper using GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ and CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite. Among the developed AC labels, patterns developed using CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite ink exhibit intense red emission under UV 365 nm light. Fig. 13b shows the developed images on various surfaces (OHP sheet, craft paper, marble, cardboard) using simple brush mode. The created AC patterns on diverse substrates exhibit intense red colour under 365 nm UV light and invisible under day light. To further evaluate the stability of the AC patterns stored at RT for various time durations (1–60 days) (Fig. 13c). Even after 60 days of aging, the emission color of the AC patterns remained unchanged. This robust and consistent red emission underscores the environmental resilience of CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ ink, confirming its suitability for secure applications. Fig. 13d illustrates the exceptional photo-stability of AC patterns before and after exposure to UV treatment (36 mW/cm2) for various durations (0–30 min). The fluorescent intensity remained stable for up to 15 min, followed by a slight decrease, indicating the excellent UV resistance of the AC tags [126]. To evaluate the stability of AC patterns under different temperatures, patterns drawn on marble are subjected to temperatures ranging from 100 to 200 °C for 30 min (Fig. 13e). Remarkably, the patterns retained their distinctiveness and consistency even when exposed to 200 °C. These results imply that CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ could potentially substitute conventional fluorescent inks due to its strong intensity, high quantum yield, solubility, excellent ink stability, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness [127]. To evaluate the chemical stability of the ink, the patterns underwent treatment with ethanol solvent, which could potentially degrade the polymeric substrate used as the base for the design [128]. The patterns exhibited significant resistance to the solvent, as illustrated by the digital photographs shown in Fig. 13f.

Fig. 14 a shows optical images of the PVA, GAO:7Eu3+, GAO:7Eu3+/Li+, CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite film under daylight and UV light. A transmission experiment is conducted to verify the film's transmittance. Different mass ratios of GAO led to changes in optical transmittance, demonstrating good transparency under daylight and red emission when excited by 365 nm UV light (Fig. 14b). It can be observed that the transmittance of the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ film decreases with the addition of more GAO phosphor. The mechanical properties of the prepared CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ film are thoroughly assessed by testing its stretchability, as depicted in Fig. 14c. Rectangular film samples are subjected to various tensile strains from 0 to 200 %. The elongation at break can be calculated using the following Eqn. (22) [129]:
(22)
e =
L a
−
L 0
L 0
In this context, e denotes the elongation at break, Lo represents the initial length of the sample, and La indicates the length of the sample at the point of breakage under tension. Generally, a higher elongation at break indicates greater softness and elasticity. The value of 200 % elongation highlights the considerable flexibility of the film. Moreover, the film exhibits the ability to return to its original shape after being stretched, demonstrating outstanding resilience and reusability in real-world applications. This characteristic is consistent with the principles of green packaging, particularly the 4R1D concept (recycle, reuse, recover, and reduce). The high elasticity of the impregnated CDs contributes to this mechanical property, further enhancing the potential application of CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ in the AC field. From a macroscopic perspective, CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ displays remarkable flexibility, allowing for easy folding and distortion, as illustrated in Fig. 14d. Additionally, its ability to conform seamlessly to surfaces of diverse shapes renders it suitable for a broad spectrum of complex scenarios that demand exceptional flexibility. These findings suggest that the structure conferred exceptional mechanical properties upon the film, implying its substantial potential and durability in practical applications [130].

To sum up, we have developed a simple and environmentally friendly method of enhancing the luminous characteristics of GAO:Eu3+/Li+ phosphors by mixing them with CDs to form CDs@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite. The increase in photoluminescence emission intensity correlates with the difference in ionic radii and charges between the co-dopant and the host cation. Phosphors co-doped with Li+ ions demonstrate higher luminescence levels compared to those co-doped with Ca2+ and Bi3+ ions. The emission intensity of the CDs(7 wt%)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is improved by 66.23 times compared to GAO:7Eu3+ phosphor, while having negligible effect on the phase structure and morphology. This rise in brightness is probably caused by FRET between CDs and luminous Eu3+, which is followed by electron capture by CDs. The Judd-Ofelt intensity parameters (Ω2 > Ω4) indicate the existence of covalent bonding between the metal and ligands, as well as the lack of symmetry around the Eu3+ ion. The substantial asymmetry ratio additionally suggests that the Eu3+ ions occupy sites with low symmetry and no inversion center. The PL intensity variation of the CDs (7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite is investigated for its temperature-sensing properties. In optical thermometry or temperature sensing applications, the CDs(7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite exhibited an outstanding Sr value of 3.26 % K−1, determined through an Ea value of 0.301 eV. This denotes a higher relative sensitivity compared to other materials. Additionally, the stability of the NCs is extensively studied in comparison to CDs across a range of temperatures. The CDs(7 wt %)@GAO:7Eu3+/Li+ composite showed impressive thermal stability of 95.6 %, making it suitable for potential applications in optical thermometry. Furthermore, the optimized composite demonstrated its potential in AC ink applications, exhibiting ultra-bright red fluorescence emission in brush mode. This indicates its effectiveness as an optical sensor for verifying the authenticity of security documents through photo sensing.
